category_summary:
The AI community was electrified by **OpenAI**'s announcement that **GPT-5.2** [derived a novel result](/?date=2026-02-14&category=social#item-aa89497361c4) in theoretical physics — a gluon interaction finding published as a preprint with **IAS**, **Harvard**, **Cambridge**, and **Vanderbilt**. Physicist **Andy Strominger** [called it potentially unsolvable](/?date=2026-02-14&category=social#item-fc7cc4f65fe9) by humans alone, marking a watershed moment for AI-assisted scientific discovery.

- **Boris Cherny** [revealed **Spotify**'s deep adoption](/?date=2026-02-14&category=social#item-9b6156ea881c) of **Claude Code**: top developers haven't written code since December and ship features from Slack, signaling a cultural shift in enterprise engineering
- **Shane Legg** (DeepMind co-founder) [updated his AGI estimates](/?date=2026-02-14&category=social#item-2fdf2c344e8a) to 50% by 2028 and 70% by 2030, while teasing upcoming AGI tests from **Google**
- **François Chollet** [pushed back](/?date=2026-02-14&category=social#item-70342e6cf2b0), arguing AGI won't trigger an intelligence explosion — scientific progress is fundamentally linear, not exponential
- **Gary Marcus** [presented data-driven evidence](/?date=2026-02-14&category=social#item-b502a78d9316) that LLM hallucinations remain unsolved across law, science, and medicine
- **Andrew Ng** [shared insights from **Sundance**](/?date=2026-02-14&category=social#item-980873eccd5f) on AI-Hollywood tensions around IP licensing, job displacement, and creative control
- **Gary Marcus** [flagged that **xAI**](/?date=2026-02-14&category=social#item-06bf8ad1a2ec) appears to have no safety team based on an org chart **Elon Musk** shared publicly

GPT-5.2 derived a novel result in theoretical physics, showing that a type of particle interaction many physicists expected would not occur can in fact arise under specific conditions.

There is great promise in the potential of AI to benefit people by accelerating science. https://t.co/B1zpYbKfcZ

items.title: I agree that AI testing is best thought of as a process and that what humans can typically do is a n...
items.content:
I agree that AI testing is best thought of as a process and that what humans can typically do is a natural minimal bar for AGI.

Perhaps I'm bit more optimistic about the speed of progress: 50% chance by 2028, 70% by 2030.

items.title: Are LLM hallucinations basically solved, as a former Senior Policy Advisor at the White House, @dean...
items.content:
Are LLM hallucinations basically solved, as a former Senior Policy Advisor at the White House, @deanwball, told me below, based pure on anecdotal experience and without data?

No. 

Instead, his post is symptomatic of how subjective finger-in-the-wind evaluations of AI often get things wrong. 

Here are some actual data.

• Law: Incidents in which lawyers have gotten busted for using fake cases are way up; @DamienCharlotin’s database listed around 100 cases less than a year ago, and now has over 900, with new incidents reported ever day.  

• Science: The situation is so bad that a team of Japanese reseachers just coined the term “hallucitation”, and showed quantitively that the problem is rapidly getting worse, not better.

• Pharma: A forthcoming study on recent models from @blueguardrails  on “challenging problems” shows hallucination rates of 26%-69%.

• @AIMultiple’s January benchmark showed rates of 15%+ across a wide range of models.

• OpenAI has started taking the problem so seriously they wrote a whole white paper about it last Fall, acknowledging that “even as models get more advanced, they can still hallucinate, confidently giving wrong answers instead of acknowledging uncertainty.”

Meanwhile, indirect measures also seem to reflect serious ongoing reliability issues on the part of current generative AI: 

• the recent Remote Labor Index showed that AI could only do 2.5% of a sample of online human tasks. (Hallucinations were surely part of why many tasks were not completed accurately.)

• Multiple studies (MIT, PwC, etc) have shown that the vast majority of companies have found little to no RoI for generative AI investments. Again, reliability is surely part of the problem.

It’s easy to look superficially at LLMs and feel like they are doing fine. Most of the errors are subtle; you have to read carefully to see them. (A fake citation, for example, looks pretty much like a real citation.)

But with careful inspection it is clear that loads of hallucinations remain, and those hallucinations remain a serious problem in the real world.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus presents a detailed, data-driven case that LLM hallucinations are NOT solved: law (fake cases up from 100 to 900+), science ('hallucitation' coined), pharma (26-69% hallucination rates), 15%+ across models, OpenAI's own white paper acknowledging the problem, plus indirect measures like low task completion rates and poor enterprise ROI.
items.reasoning: Comprehensive, data-rich post from a credible critic. Multiple cited studies and databases. High engagement (24K views). Important counternarrative to claims that hallucinations are solved.

Here are some actual data.

• Pharma: A forthcoming study on recent models from @blueguardrails on “challenging problems” shows hallucination rates of 26%-69%.

• OpenAI has started taking the problem so seriously they wrote a whole white paper about it last Fall, acknowledging that “even as models get more advanced, they can still hallucinate, confidently giving wrong answers instead of acknowledging uncertainty.”

items.title: The authors of the preprint realized this a year ago and sought to find the correct formula for inte...
items.content:
The authors of the preprint realized this a year ago and sought to find the correct formula for interactions involving any number n of gluons, going up to n=6 by hand but obtaining complicated expressions that they sought to simplify, without success. 

GPT-5.2 simplified these expressions and then conjectured a simple formula for the general case. 

Next, a separate scaffolded internal OpenAI model spent roughly 12 hours reasoning through the problem, independently deriving the same formula and producing a formal proof. 

The final result was then verified by the authors.

items.summary: OpenAI details how GPT-5.2 simplified complex gluon interaction expressions that physicists couldn't simplify by hand, conjectured a general formula, and a separate scaffolded model independently derived and formally proved the same formula in ~12 hours.

GPT-5.2 not only simplified expressions but conjectured a general formula, with an independent AI-generated formal proof. Very high engagement (580 likes, 99K views). This is the most technically informative tweet in the thread.

items.title: I recently spoke at the Sundance Film Festival on a panel about AI. Sundance is an annual gathering ...
items.content:
I recently spoke at the Sundance Film Festival on a panel about AI. Sundance is an annual gathering of filmmakers and movie buffs that serves as the premier showcase for independent films in the United States. Knowing that many people in Hollywood are extremely uncomfortable about AI, I decided to immerse myself for a day in this community to learn about their anxieties and build bridges.

I’m grateful to Daniel Dae Kim @danieldaekim, an actor/producer/director I’ve come to respect deeply for his artistic and social work, for organizing the panel, which also included Daniel, Dan Kwan, Jonathan Wang, and Janet Yang. I found myself surrounded by award-winning filmmakers and definitely felt like the odd person out!

First, Hollywood has many reasons to be uncomfortable with AI. People from the entertainment industry come from a very different culture than many who work in tech, and this drives deep differences in what we focus on and what we value. A significant subset of Hollywood is concerned that:
- AI companies are taking their work to learn from it without consent and compensation. Whereas the software industry is used to open source and the open internet, Hollywood focuses much more on intellectual property, which underlies the core economic engines of the entertainment industry.
- Powerful unions like SAG-AFTRA (Screen Actors Guild-American Federation of Television and Radio Artists) are deeply concerned about protecting the jobs of their members. When AI technology (or any other force) threatens the livelihoods of their members — like voice actors — they will fight mightily against potential job losses.
- This wave of technological change feels forced on them more than previous waves, where they felt more free to adopt or reject the technology. For example, celebrities felt like it was up to them whether to use social media. In contrast, negative messaging from some AI leaders who present the technology as unstoppable, perhaps even a dangerous force that will wipe out many jobs, has not encouraged enthusiastic adoption.

Having said that, Hollywood is under no illusions that AI will change entertainment, and that if Hollywood does not adapt, perhaps some other place will become the new center for entertainment. The entertainment industry is no stranger to technology change. Radio, TV, computer graphics special effects, video streaming, and social media transformed the industry. But the path to navigating AI’s transformation is still unclear, and organizations like the new Creators Coalition on AI are trying to stake out positions. Unfortunately, Hollywood’s negative sentiment toward AI also means it will produce a lot more Terminator-like movies that portray AI as more dangerous than helpful, and this hurts beneficial AI adoption as well.

The interests of AI and Hollywood are not always aligned. (Every time I speak in a group like this as the “AI representative,” I can count on being asked very hard questions.) Most of us in tech would prefer a more open internet and more permissive use of creative works. But there is also much common ground, for example in wanting guardrails against deepfakes and a smooth transition for those whose jobs are displaced, perhaps via upskilling.

Storytelling is hard. I’m optimistic that AI tools like Veo, Sora, Runway, Kling, Ray, Hailuo, and many others can make video creation easier for millions of people. I hope Hollywood and AI developers will find more opportunities to collaborate, find more common ground, and also steer our projects toward outcomes that are win-win for as many parties as possible.

[Original text: https://t.co/70dgCmdxtQ ]

items.title: “‘Safety is a dead org at xAI,’ he said. Looking at the restructured org chart Elon Musk shared on X...
items.content:
“‘Safety is a dead org at xAI,’ he said. Looking at the restructured org chart Elon Musk shared on X, there’s no mention of a safety team.” 

Last July I wrote about how Elon Musk had personally elevated my p(doom), from very low to low.

This latest development is a natural extension of that fear. A zillionaire hell bent on winning the AI race and no longer interested in AI safety is not what the world needs.

items.title: I don't think the rise of AGI will lead to a sudden exponential explosion in AI capabilities. There ...
items.content: I don't think the rise of AGI will lead to a sudden exponential explosion in AI capabilities. There are bottlenecks on the sources of new capability improvements, and horizontally scaling intelligence in silicon (even by a massive factor) doesn't lift those bottlenecks.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues AGI won't lead to a sudden exponential explosion in capabilities because bottlenecks on capability improvements can't be lifted by horizontally scaling intelligence in silicon.
items.reasoning: Major contrarian thesis from highly credible AI researcher (Keras creator, ARC prize). 21K views, 302 likes. Directly challenges singularity/foom narratives.

items.title:
Once there was a planet with a huge asteroid heading toward it.

Stopping the asteroid would have re...
items.content:
Once there was a planet with a huge asteroid heading toward it.

Stopping the asteroid would have required a few large countries to cooperate a moderate amount.  That seemed hard.  Some people became worried.

A cult arose which said the asteroid would grant its believers eternal life, when it struck the planet.

Nobody knew how to make the asteroid do that.  But the cult said you couldn't prove it wouldn't.  So there was no need to worry, and you could set your mind at ease.

They called it the Asteroid of Immortality.

Some of the world's most famous astronomers tried to explain in more detail what would happen when the asteroid smashed into the planet, and that it didn't involve eternal life.

The cult said that nobody had seen that disaster actually happen, so it wasn't scientific to believe in it.

(Other astronomers joined the cult of the Asteroid of Immortality.  It regarded astronomers who joined them very favorably and warmly -- the cult did, that is; not the asteroid.)

"If the asteroid *doesn't* hit our planet, everyone dies!" said the cult.  "Like, because of old age, get it?  Ha ha!"  They thought this reply very clever.

Transhumanists tried to point out that cryonics was in fact a thing, if somebody was that desperate to grasp any chance of escaping death by old age; that you could desperately grasp at immortality *without* endangering all life on the planet.

Skeptics tried to explain that putting your faith in a falling asteroid to save you, just because it seemed big and powerful, wasn't much of a chance to grasp however desperately, because a falling asteroid would actually just kill you.

People who cared about something other than themselves, tried to say that it was different for everyone to all die at the same time, including everyone's children; and leave no legacy for the children's children who might have been.

"Everyone will die," said those trying to rally the world, "including your children; or your friends' children, if you've none of your own; they'll die before they have a chance to grow up, and have lives or children of their own.  Every story ends in time; that's not the same as ending all stories."

"Everyone will die all at the same time, if we don't stop the asteroid, and that will be the end."

This didn't work to talk most believers out of their faith.  Thinking it clever to reply "Ah ha ha, but everyone dies even if the asteroid *doesn't* hit!" usually meant having too little wisdom to understand the counter-replies.  If you couldn't figure out the problems for yourself, before your mouth uttered such words, you usually wouldn't recant when somebody else tried to explain.

Instead the cult decided to call the anti-doom coalition "doomers", and thought that very clever too.

The cult spent vast amounts to build huge electromagnets to try to pull in the asteroid faster.  The cult knew, their faith held, that the asteroid would fall in time regardless.  But the prospect of pulling down the asteroid a little sooner, let them feel powerful and in control, and like *they* were the ones making history.

(Indeed, many splinter factions within the cult each said that if their followers invested enough to build the *most* powerful magnet, that would make it be *their* Asteroid of Immortality, and *they* would become the rulers of the new world.)

Above all, the cult worked to stoke enmity between the couple of large countries that would have needed to work together to deflect the asteroid.

And at that task, unfortunately, the cult succeeded.  For it was ever easier to push people downhill than uphill, to fight alongside entropy rather than fighting back against it; and call the default sad outcome your victory.  Coordination was hard and not the default, and maybe it wouldn't have happened either way.  But the cult did fight on the side of entropy, and entropy did win.

The cult likewise succeeded at pulling down the asteroid with electromagnets, if you wanted to look at things that way.  They got the default outcome they'd defined as their own victory.  They managed to let a falling asteroid fall.

And then everyone died, all at the same time including all the children, and that was the end of all stories.

items.content_html:
Once there was a planet with a huge asteroid heading toward it.

Stopping the asteroid would have required a few large countries to cooperate a moderate amount. That seemed hard. Some people became worried.

A cult arose which said the asteroid would grant its believers eternal life, when it struck the planet.

Nobody knew how to make the asteroid do that. But the cult said you couldn't prove it wouldn't. So there was no need to worry, and you could set your mind at ease.

They called it the Asteroid of Immortality.

Some of the world's most famous astronomers tried to explain in more detail what would happen when the asteroid smashed into the planet, and that it didn't involve eternal life.

The cult said that nobody had seen that disaster actually happen, so it wasn't scientific to believe in it.

(Other astronomers joined the cult of the Asteroid of Immortality. It regarded astronomers who joined them very favorably and warmly -- the cult did, that is; not the asteroid.)

"If the asteroid *doesn't* hit our planet, everyone dies!" said the cult. "Like, because of old age, get it? Ha ha!" They thought this reply very clever.

Transhumanists tried to point out that cryonics was in fact a thing, if somebody was that desperate to grasp any chance of escaping death by old age; that you could desperately grasp at immortality *without* endangering all life on the planet.

Skeptics tried to explain that putting your faith in a falling asteroid to save you, just because it seemed big and powerful, wasn't much of a chance to grasp however desperately, because a falling asteroid would actually just kill you.

People who cared about something other than themselves, tried to say that it was different for everyone to all die at the same time, including everyone's children; and leave no legacy for the children's children who might have been.

"Everyone will die," said those trying to rally the world, "including your children; or your friends' children, if you've none of your own; they'll die before they have a chance to grow up, and have lives or children of their own. Every story ends in time; that's not the same as ending all stories."

"Everyone will die all at the same time, if we don't stop the asteroid, and that will be the end."

This didn't work to talk most believers out of their faith. Thinking it clever to reply "Ah ha ha, but everyone dies even if the asteroid *doesn't* hit!" usually meant having too little wisdom to understand the counter-replies. If you couldn't figure out the problems for yourself, before your mouth uttered such words, you usually wouldn't recant when somebody else tried to explain.

Instead the cult decided to call the anti-doom coalition "doomers", and thought that very clever too.

The cult spent vast amounts to build huge electromagnets to try to pull in the asteroid faster. The cult knew, their faith held, that the asteroid would fall in time regardless. But the prospect of pulling down the asteroid a little sooner, let them feel powerful and in control, and like *they* were the ones making history.

(Indeed, many splinter factions within the cult each said that if their followers invested enough to build the *most* powerful magnet, that would make it be *their* Asteroid of Immortality, and *they* would become the rulers of the new world.)

Above all, the cult worked to stoke enmity between the couple of large countries that would have needed to work together to deflect the asteroid.

And at that task, unfortunately, the cult succeeded. For it was ever easier to push people downhill than uphill, to fight alongside entropy rather than fighting back against it; and call the default sad outcome your victory. Coordination was hard and not the default, and maybe it wouldn't have happened either way. But the cult did fight on the side of entropy, and entropy did win.

The cult likewise succeeded at pulling down the asteroid with electromagnets, if you wanted to look at things that way. They got the default outcome they'd defined as their own victory. They managed to let a falling asteroid fall.

And then everyone died, all at the same time including all the children, and that was the end of all stories.

One of the most impactful things that OpenAI could do to make more people understand how good AI has become is to TELL THEM when they are given to a weak model. 

The router is incredibly confusing to almost everyone and people don’t get that there is no such thing as ChatGPT-5.2

We ❤️ launches! Here’s a recap of what went out this week: 

— An upgrade to Gemini 3 Deep Think tha...
items.content:
We ❤️ launches! Here’s a recap of what went out this week: 

— An upgrade to Gemini 3 Deep Think that solves complex modern science and engineering challenges. Scientists, researchers, and enterprises can express interest in Deep Think via our early access program and Google AI Ultra subscribers can access it in @GeminiApp 

 — The @Waymo World Model, which is powered by Genie 3 and allows the autonomous Waymo Driver to master complex scenarios long before it sees them on the road 

— A trio of top feature requests for @StitchbyGoogle - direct export to Figma as editable layers, the research-driven Ideate Agent, and Design Systems for cross-project style consistency

— Perch 2.0, an update to our bioacoustics foundation model, that advances our ability to disentangle underwater sounds (like whale vocalizations!) and monitor marine ecosystems

— Video Overviews in the @NotebookLM mobile app, allowing you to learn anything on the go

— The @Waymo World Model, which is powered by Genie 3 and allows the autonomous Waymo Driver to master complex scenarios long before it sees them on the road

— A trio of top feature requests for @StitchbyGoogle - direct export to Figma as editable layers, the research-driven Ideate Agent, and Design Systems for cross-project style consistency

— Perch 2.0, an update to our bioacoustics foundation model, that advances our ability to disentangle underwater sounds (like whale vocalizations!) and monitor marine ecosystems

— Video Overviews in the @NotebookLM mobile app, allowing you to learn anything on the go

items.title: That process, if you zoom out, is not exponential (though it does involved many lower-level exponent...
items.content:
That process, if you zoom out, is not exponential (though it does involved many lower-level exponentials, mostly at the level of system inputs).

It is essentially linear. The weight/importance of scientific progress over, say, 1850-1900 is comparable to 1900-1950, 1950-2000, or 2000-2050.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues that scientific progress is essentially linear, not exponential — each 50-year period from 1850-2050 has comparable weight/importance.
items.reasoning: Substantive and contrarian claim about the pace of scientific progress, from a highly credible source. Good engagement (10K views).

items.title: When I posted the below post, I forgot how token cheap altman is. After just one day of work with Co...
items.content:
When I posted the below post, I forgot how token cheap altman is. After just one day of work with Codex 5.3, it told me that I exhausted all the available tokens on my Plus plan and I can go fuck myself for 3 days or pay $200/month for a Pro plan.

I decided that Altman can go fuck himself instead, so I'm back to Claude Code CLI (Pro) and Antigravity (Free).

While I can see that Gemini with Antigravity isn't as strong as Claude Code or Codex, but its agentic and fast, so I don't see any issue using it. And the two combined cost me only $17 a month for Anthropic. So far, I haven't hit the limit after almost a day of work.

items.title: It's been an incredible roller coaster for me with the AI agents this week, that finished on a down ...
items.content: It's been an incredible roller coaster for me with the AI agents this week, that finished on a down note. I still don't think these agents understand even the *rudimentary* aspects of that they are actually doing. Sure, if there is a very straightforward *pattern* to doing some complex task, they can be very useful and productive. But as soon as you want to do something nontrivial that requires deeper understanding of the task, you are essentially playing a roulette and need to engage in a massive amount of hand holding and revisions.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz shares critical assessment of AI agents after a week of use: they don't understand rudimentary aspects of what they're doing, only work on straightforward patterns, and require massive hand-holding for nontrivial tasks.
items.reasoning: High engagement (134 likes, 10K views) and substantive original insight from a respected practitioner. Provides a nuanced, experience-based critique of current AI agent capabilities that contrasts with hype narratives.
items.summary_html: Tunguz shares critical assessment of AI agents after a week of use: they don't understand rudimentary aspects of what they're doing, only work on straightforward patterns, and require massive hand-holding for nontrivial tasks.
items.content_html: It's been an incredible roller coaster for me with the AI agents this week, that finished on a down note. I still don't think these agents understand even the *rudimentary* aspects of that they are actually doing. Sure, if there is a very straightforward *pattern* to doing some complex task, they can be very useful and productive. But as soon as you want to do something nontrivial that requires deeper understanding of the task, you are essentially playing a roulette and need to engage in a massive amount of hand holding and revisions.

items.title: 🚀 DeepSeek R1 on GB300 with vLLM: 22.5K prefill TGS and 3K decode TGS per GPU — an 8x prefill and 10...
items.content:
🚀 DeepSeek R1 on GB300 with vLLM: 22.5K prefill TGS and 3K decode TGS per GPU — an 8x prefill and 10-20x mixed-context improvement over Hopper.

DeepSeek V3.2 on 2 GPUs (NVFP4 + TP2): 7.4K prefill TGS and 2.8K decode TGS.

Key recipe:
⚡ NVFP4 weights from HuggingFace
⚡ FlashInfer FP4 MoE kernel (`VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_MOE_FP4=1`)
⚡ 2 GPUs is all you need — 288GB HBM per GPU

EP vs TP, MTP tuning, disaggregated prefill — all covered.

🙏 Collab with @daocloud_io & @verdacloud 

🔗 Blog: https://t.co/i5Nz5SeV5Y
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM shares benchmark results for DeepSeek R1 on GB300 GPUs: 8x prefill and 10-20x decode improvement over Hopper. Also shows DeepSeek V3.2 running on just 2 GPUs with NVFP4.
items.reasoning: Important infrastructure benchmarks showing GB300 performance gains for DeepSeek models. Practical deployment info (2 GPUs for V3.2). Good technical detail.
items.summary_html: vLLM shares benchmark results for DeepSeek R1 on GB300 GPUs: 8x prefill and 10-20x decode improvement over Hopper. Also shows DeepSeek V3.2 running on just 2 GPUs with NVFP4.

In modern amplitude theory, simple formulas often signal deeper structure. 

Showing that a case lon...
items.content:
In modern amplitude theory, simple formulas often signal deeper structure. 

Showing that a case long thought to be empty actually contains structure sharpens our understanding of the mathematics of the strong force and opens new directions, including extensions to gravity and related amplitude relations. 

Some of these generalizations are also amenable to solution via this new AI methodology and will be reported on elsewhere.

items.title: For decades, one specific gluon interaction (“single-minus” at tree level) was widely treated as hav...
items.content:
For decades, one specific gluon interaction (“single-minus” at tree level) was widely treated as having zero amplitude, meaning it was assumed not to occur. 

When an amplitude is zero, physicists may ignore it. But this preprint shows that the conclusion is too strong: in a carefully defined situation — where the particles’ motions satisfy a specific alignment condition — the amplitude is not zero.
items.author: @OpenAI
items.summary: OpenAI explains the physics context: a gluon interaction previously assumed to have zero amplitude actually has non-zero amplitude under specific alignment conditions.
items.reasoning: Important scientific context for the discovery, high engagement (118K views).

items.content_html:
For decades, one specific gluon interaction (“single-minus” at tree level) was widely treated as having zero amplitude, meaning it was assumed not to occur.

When an amplitude is zero, physicists may ignore it. But this preprint shows that the conclusion is too strong: in a carefully defined situation — where the particles’ motions satisfy a specific alignment condition — the amplitude is not zero.

items.title: Vanilla Transformer-based LLMs are slow at generating text because, at each step, they need to load ...
items.content:
Vanilla Transformer-based LLMs are slow at generating text because, at each step, they need to load from memory a set of cached values (called "keys" and "values") that help the model decide what to pay attention to from earlier in the conversation.

A previous technique called multi-query attention (MQA) shrinks this cache by sharing a single set of keys and values across all the model's parallel attention computations (called "heads"), which speeds things up a lot but hurts output quality.

This landmark paper, published by Google researchers, introduces grouped-query attention (GQA), which splits the attention heads into a few groups, each sharing their own set of keys and values — so you get most of the speed benefit without losing much quality.

On top of that, the authors show you don't need to train a model with GQA from scratch: you can take an existing vanilla model that was trained the normal way, average together its key and value weights into the grouped structure, and then do a short round of additional training using only about 5% of the original training cost.

The results show that this converted model nearly matches the quality of the original while running almost as fast as the most aggressive compression.

It's one of those papers where a relatively simple idea had an outsized practical impact — it changed how nearly all major open-weight LLMs are built within months of publication, and it remains the default attention configuration for production models today.

Read the paper with an AI tutor: https://t.co/VX5TTPex8m

Read it alone: https://t.co/f3edG4dcng
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov provides an educational deep-dive on Grouped-Query Attention (GQA), explaining how it balances cache efficiency and quality compared to vanilla attention and MQA, and how existing models can be cheaply converted. Notes its outsized practical impact on LLM architecture.
items.reasoning: Excellent educational content about a foundational LLM architecture technique from a known ML educator. Well-explained technical content, though GQA is an established technique rather than breaking news.
items.summary_html: Burkov provides an educational deep-dive on Grouped-Query Attention (GQA), explaining how it balances cache efficiency and quality compared to vanilla attention and MQA, and how existing models can be cheaply converted. Notes its outsized practical impact on LLM architecture.
items.content_html:
Vanilla Transformer-based LLMs are slow at generating text because, at each step, they need to load from memory a set of cached values (called "keys" and "values") that help the model decide what to pay attention to from earlier in the conversation.

A previous technique called multi-query attention (MQA) shrinks this cache by sharing a single set of keys and values across all the model's parallel attention computations (called "heads"), which speeds things up a lot but hurts output quality.

This landmark paper, published by Google researchers, introduces grouped-query attention (GQA), which splits the attention heads into a few groups, each sharing their own set of keys and values — so you get most of the speed benefit without losing much quality.

On top of that, the authors show you don't need to train a model with GQA from scratch: you can take an existing vanilla model that was trained the normal way, average together its key and value weights into the grouped structure, and then do a short round of additional training using only about 5% of the original training cost.

The results show that this converted model nearly matches the quality of the original while running almost as fast as the most aggressive compression.

It's one of those papers where a relatively simple idea had an outsized practical impact — it changed how nearly all major open-weight LLMs are built within months of publication, and it remains the default attention configuration for production models today.

items.title:
Previously, Google Search dominated in:

1. Index freshness
2. Speed of search
3. Accuracy of search...
items.content:
Previously, Google Search dominated in:

1. Index freshness
2. Speed of search
3. Accuracy of search

The search accuracy here is the chance of putting the right result among the top 3 search results.

LLMs made the second and third advantage irrelevant. As long as the right result is in the top 20, answering the user's question will be quick enough. And the competitors are already doing that.

Competing only on index freshness isn't a viable strategy because it's not a huge moat and the competitors are quickly catching up.

So, not only is Google's index getting increasingly polluted by the AI slop, which is unfixable, it can no longer compete in anything the competitors don't know how to do right.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues Google Search's three advantages (freshness, speed, accuracy) are being eroded: LLMs made speed/accuracy less important (top-20 results suffice), and index freshness alone isn't a sufficient moat, especially as AI slop pollutes the index.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful analysis of Google Search's competitive position in the LLM era from a knowledgeable ML practitioner. Good strategic reasoning about how AI changes search dynamics.

items.title: excited to follow up our Gemini Deep Think coverage of @yitayml and now @JeffDean, and in my other c...
items.content:
excited to follow up our Gemini Deep Think coverage of @yitayml and now @JeffDean, and in my other capacity, host @_philschmid and other fantastic @GoogleDeepMind colleagues at AIE Europe!

my favorite part of the Jeff conversation was how he gamely handled everything I could think of, including my cheeky “Where is Gemini Ultra?”, how suddenly everyone solved @GregKamradt’s NIAH context challenge, and I even got him to mock up “Numbers Every AI Engineer Should Know”, with a surprising answer that he thinks about everything from chip design to the relationship of model arch to memory transfer in AI in terms of energy (picojoules) rather than time (ms) anymore — makes sense bc AI infra is clustered rather than distributed!
items.author: @swyx
items.summary: Swyx promotes his interviews with Jeff Dean and other Google DeepMind staff at AIE Europe. Highlights Jeff Dean's perspective on measuring AI infrastructure in terms of energy (picojoules) rather than time, and discussions about Gemini Ultra and context challenges.
items.reasoning: Substantive interview highlights with Jeff Dean. The energy-vs-time framing for AI infrastructure is a notable insight. Good engagement (147 likes, 12K views).
items.summary_html: Swyx promotes his interviews with Jeff Dean and other Google DeepMind staff at AIE Europe. Highlights Jeff Dean's perspective on measuring AI infrastructure in terms of energy (picojoules) rather than time, and discussions about Gemini Ultra and context challenges.
items.content_html:
excited to follow up our Gemini Deep Think coverage of @yitayml and now @JeffDean, and in my other capacity, host @_philschmid and other fantastic @GoogleDeepMind colleagues at AIE Europe!

my favorite part of the Jeff conversation was how he gamely handled everything I could think of, including my cheeky “Where is Gemini Ultra?”, how suddenly everyone solved @GregKamradt’s NIAH context challenge, and I even got him to mock up “Numbers Every AI Engineer Should Know”, with a surprising answer that he thinks about everything from chip design to the relationship of model arch to memory transfer in AI in terms of energy (picojoules) rather than time (ms) anymore — makes sense bc AI infra is clustered rather than distributed!

items.title:
Open source is the opposite of what every big AI lab is offering.

I was using the Cline CLI for the...
items.content:
Open source is the opposite of what every big AI lab is offering.

I was using the Cline CLI for the first time, and they offer the freedom you won't get with any of the proprietary agentic coding tools:

• It's open-source
• You aren't locked into a single model
• Bring your own API Key to use any model
• Over 5 million developers already use it
• Fastest-growing AI open-source project on GitHub

Even better:

Right now, you can use two of the best open source models for free: MiniMax M2.5 and Kimi K2.5.

To install it: npm install -g cline

items.title: When chatbots were trained to be helpful, human-like chat assistants we had a good world model for h...
items.content:
When chatbots were trained to be helpful, human-like chat assistants we had a good world model for how they'd react to google-fu and other broken instructions.

As agents become extremely powerful, we need to develop a new skill of providing instructions. Models with the best instruction following abilities should make it much easier to explore the frontier here.

There's potential something deeper in this.

items.title: A fundamental flaw with this research is that they went to a remote work website where customers pla...
items.content:
A fundamental flaw with this research is that they went to a remote work website where customers place jobs that they haven't succeeded in automating with AI to test whether AI can do these jobs better than humans. Well, no, it cannot, because it's a remote work website where customers place jobs that they haven't succeeded in automating with AI.

Another less fundamental flaw is that they used humans to evaluate whether AI did a better job than a human in a non-blind setting, so human raters would unavoidably be biased.

Finally, the definition of AI failure as the quality of its deliverables being lower than that of a human worker is doubtful. The quality is usually a function of cost, so if by using AI, you can solve the problem with lower quality but at a lower cost, it still might be a viable solution for many customers.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov critiques a research study on AI vs human workers, noting selection bias (jobs on the platform were already ones AI couldn't automate), non-blind human evaluation bias, and flawed quality-only comparison ignoring cost tradeoffs.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful methodological critique of AI labor replacement research from a known ML author. Good analytical points about study design flaws.
items.summary_html: Burkov critiques a research study on AI vs human workers, noting selection bias (jobs on the platform were already ones AI couldn't automate), non-blind human evaluation bias, and flawed quality-only comparison ignoring cost tradeoffs.
items.content_html:
A fundamental flaw with this research is that they went to a remote work website where customers place jobs that they haven't succeeded in automating with AI to test whether AI can do these jobs better than humans. Another less fundamental flaw is that they used humans to evaluate whether AI did a better job than a human in a non-blind setting, so human raters would unavoidably be biased.

Finally, the definition of AI failure as the quality of its deliverables being lower than that of a human worker is doubtful. The quality is usually a function of cost, so if by using AI, you can solve the problem with lower quality but at a lower cost, it still might be a viable solution for many customers.

items.title: Before OpenClaw, when I said that they are currently polluting the Web with AI slop, I used "they" w...
items.content:
Before OpenClaw, when I said that they are currently polluting the Web with AI slop, I used "they" without attributing the blame to anyone in particular. In my head, "they" were some shady corporations or people who know how to use LLMs to generate this content, and then they have some platforms—like content farms—to distribute it.

Now, when I say that the Web is currently being puked and shat into, I know exactly who is doing this, how they do it, where they do it, and how many of them there are.

This is officially the beginning of the end of the Web as we know it, and we know when and by whom this end has started.🦞🦀🕸️🪦

items.summary: Burkov discusses how OpenClaw has made it clear exactly who is polluting the web with AI slop, calling it 'the beginning of the end of the Web as we know it.'

items.title:
re: app layer > model layer

one structural advantage of why agent labs/open source hackers like @op...
items.content:
re: app layer > model layer

one structural advantage of why agent labs/open source hackers like @openclaw can run rings around the infinite money glitched model labs of the world is 

1) they can simply argmax(all model labs) 
2) they can yolo permissions without safetyniks being able to block anything

as vocal a fan i am of claude cowork, i think this is a nice small area where non-biglab people can outwork and outexplore capabilities.
items.author: @swyx
items.summary: Swyx argues app-layer/open-source agent labs like OpenClaw have structural advantages over model labs: they can pick the best model across all providers and ship without safety restrictions. Notes this as an area where non-big-lab developers can outperform.
items.reasoning: Insightful structural analysis of app-layer vs model-layer competitive dynamics. Good point about argmax over model providers and permissionless innovation.

items.title: @deanwball I think hallucinations should an issue of concern for anyone who uses LLMs, but agree peo...
items.content:
@deanwball I think hallucinations should an issue of concern for anyone who uses LLMs, but agree people’s mileage vary, as a function of task, model, exact details of training set and prompt, etc.

one of the trickiest things about LLMs is that they are better at some things than others but there is perfect way to anticipate which in advance, and dodgy output can “look” superficially just as good as sound output, to the untutored eye.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus discusses the trickiness of LLM hallucinations — they vary by task, model, and prompt, and dodgy output can look just as good as sound output to the untutored eye.
items.reasoning: Nuanced point about hallucination unpredictability, moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Marcus discusses the trickiness of LLM hallucinations — they vary by task, model, and prompt, and dodgy output can look just as good as sound output to the untutored eye.
items.content_html:
@deanwball I think hallucinations should an issue of concern for anyone who uses LLMs, but agree people’s mileage vary, as a function of task, model, exact details of training set and prompt, etc.

one of the trickiest things about LLMs is that they are better at some things than others but there is perfect way to anticipate which in advance, and dodgy output can “look” superficially just as good as sound output, to the untutored eye.

items.title: @nlpnyc You should read it some day, it's a good read. Every single thing said there is still true, ...
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet references his earlier writing about AGI being a step on a 'visibly linear ladder of progress', reaffirming that AGI continues the process of Science and proceeds at a linear pace.
items.reasoning: Restatement of Chollet's linear progress thesis. Decent engagement. Supports his main thread.
items.summary_html: Chollet references his earlier writing about AGI being a step on a 'visibly linear ladder of progress', reaffirming that AGI continues the process of Science and proceeds at a linear pace.
items.content_html:
@nlpnyc You should read it some day, it's a good read. Every single thing said there is still true, which you would know if you had actually cared to read it. This is the key bit:

> the first superhuman AI will just be another step on a visibly linear ladder of progress, that we started climbing long ago

AGI is in the continuity of the process of Science at large (which is itself a recursively self-improving intelligent process), and that process has been moving roughly at a linear pace, due to the reasons detailed in the article.

items.title:
Honestly, only nerds care about coding agents.

What your mom, friends, and neighbors want is *somet...
items.content:
Honestly, only nerds care about coding agents.

What your mom, friends, and neighbors want is *something* that does real things for them.

Lindy is a pretty dope assistant.

Nothing to install, and nothing to configure. You don't need a technical cousin to run this for you.

Here is a quick list of what it does:

• It checks your email
• It can reply back using your voice
• It schedules meetings
• It pulls context from past conversations before meetings
• It takes notes during meetings
• It tells you what's important
• It can draft and send follow-ups

It can text you on iMessage when it needs you.

This is the type of product that will show everyone the power of AI by doing real work for regular people using the tools they already use.

Here is the link: https://t.co/ZJhm2L7M0v

Thanks to the team for collaborating with me on this post.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago (svpino) promoting Lindy AI assistant - highlights features like email management, meeting scheduling, note-taking, iMessage integration. Argues real consumer-facing AI assistants matter more than coding agents.
items.reasoning: Sponsored/partnership content but raises interesting point about consumer AI vs developer-focused tools. Decent engagement. The framing that 'only nerds care about coding agents' is a notable contrarian take.
items.summary_html: Santiago (svpino) promoting Lindy AI assistant - highlights features like email management, meeting scheduling, note-taking, iMessage integration. Argues real consumer-facing AI assistants matter more than coding agents.

It can text you on iMessage when it needs you.

This is the type of product that will show everyone the power of AI by doing real work for regular people using the tools they already use.

Here is the link: https://t.co/ZJhm2L7M0v

Thanks to the team for collaborating with me on this post.

items.title: @mikebundy @openclaw Yeah, that's one of the interesting things about the OpenClaw revolution. We're...
items.content:
@mikebundy @openclaw Yeah, that's one of the interesting things about the OpenClaw revolution. We're keeping our private data from being sold to others for advertising purposes, and we're also keeping our private knowledge to ourselves so that we can use it without being monetized by the big AI companies.

Of course, that requires running local models, which is a little bit nerdy. Most people don't have something like Alex Fenn's $10,000 machine to run a big model, but this is a new world and it's real interesting to watch it evolve.
items.author: @Scobleizer

items.title: @Croc_411 Merely having tools or language is not enough to kickstart recursive self-improvement. The...
items.content:
@Croc_411 Merely having tools or language is not enough to kickstart recursive self-improvement. The total set of prerequisites were only assembled very recently. In particular:

- Writing
- Scalable publishing
- Sufficient social freedom to think and communicate about nature/people
- Food no longer a major bottleneck

Basically we had to wait until the 1700s for everything to line up.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues that recursive self-improvement required specific prerequisites (writing, scalable publishing, social freedom, food surplus) that only aligned in the 1700s.
items.reasoning: Interesting historical argument about prerequisites for intelligence takeoff, but low-engagement reply.
items.summary_html: Chollet argues that recursive self-improvement required specific prerequisites (writing, scalable publishing, social freedom, food surplus) that only aligned in the 1700s.
items.content_html:
@Croc_411 Merely having tools or language is not enough to kickstart recursive self-improvement. The total set of prerequisites were only assembled very recently. In particular:

  • Writing
  • Scalable publishing
  • Sufficient social freedom to think and communicate about nature/people
  • Food no longer a major bottleneck

Basically we had to wait until the 1700s for everything to line up.

items.content:
Merely *knowing* that an outcome you want is *attainable* leads you to automatically filter out decisions that clearly wouldn't lead to it, thus dramatically increasing the probability you'll reach it.

Belief is destiny.

✅ OlmixBase: a data mixing method informed by a comprehensive empirical study of key design choices

items.title:
We built Olmix to address two pain points we hit while training Olmo 3: 
1️⃣ There's little guidance...
items.content:
We built Olmix to address two pain points we hit while training Olmo 3: 
1️⃣ There's little guidance on how to configure mixing methods—you're left guessing at key choices 
2️⃣ Training data evolves constantly, and recomputing mixes from scratch becomes a tax on every update
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: AI2 explains Olmix motivation: addressing lack of guidance on mixing method configuration and the cost of recomputing mixes when training data changes
items.reasoning: Articulates real pain points in LM development.
items.summary_html: AI2 explains Olmix motivation: addressing lack of guidance on mixing method configuration and the cost of recomputing mixes when training data changes
items.content_html:
We built Olmix to address two pain points we hit while training Olmo 3:

1️⃣ There's little guidance on how to configure mixing methods—you're left guessing at key choices

2️⃣ Training data evolves constantly, and recomputing mixes from scratch becomes a tax on every update

items.title: Gluons carry the strong nuclear force, which is the force that binds quarks together inside protons ...
items.content:
Gluons carry the strong nuclear force, which is the force that binds quarks together inside protons and neutrons. 

Without the strong force, atomic nuclei would not exist. 

It is one of the four fundamental forces of nature and a core part of the Standard Model of particle physics.
items.author: @OpenAI
items.summary: OpenAI provides background on gluons and the strong nuclear force as context for their discovery announcement.
items.reasoning: Educational/contextual tweet, not new information for technical audience.
items.summary_html: OpenAI provides background on gluons and the strong nuclear force as context for their discovery announcement.
items.content_html:
Gluons carry the strong nuclear force, which is the force that binds quarks together inside protons and neutrons.

Without the strong force, atomic nuclei would not exist.

It is one of the four fundamental forces of nature and a core part of the Standard Model of particle physics.

items.title: Mixture reuse is our mechanism where we reuse the relative ratios of the unchanged domains and only ...
items.content: Mixture reuse is our mechanism where we reuse the relative ratios of the unchanged domains and only recompute on the domains that change. Fewer domains to recompute → lower costs as domains are updated throughout development.

items.title:
OlmixBase emerged from studying a common mixing method pattern where you ↓
1️⃣ Train a bunch of smal...
items.content:
OlmixBase emerged from studying a common mixing method pattern where you ↓
1️⃣ Train a bunch of smaller proxy models on different mixes
2️⃣ Fit a regression model to the mixture weights & performance 
3️⃣ Return the mix that optimizes predicted performance
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: AI2 explains OlmixBase methodology: train proxy models on different mixes, fit regression, optimize predicted performance
items.reasoning: Technical methodology detail.
items.summary_html: AI2 explains OlmixBase methodology: train proxy models on different mixes, fit regression, optimize predicted performance
items.content_html:
OlmixBase emerged from studying a common mixing method pattern where you ↓

1️⃣ Train a bunch of smaller proxy models on different mixes

2️⃣ Fit a regression model to the mixture weights & performance

3️⃣ Return the mix that optimizes predicted performance

items.content:
@FeelGoodEatGood Oh, I know all about the dangers of BCIs. I've done a thousand shows with a brain-computer interface pioneer. I know it can take away your free will, I know that it can listen to your inner dialogue, and I know that it might get you to see reality completely differently—to the point where you don't even know whether you're seeing reality or not.

But I'm still buying it.

items.title:
@kristofcreative What about the humans who use AI to do war?

For instance, I'm talking to you as a ...
items.content:
@kristofcreative What about the humans who use AI to do war?

For instance, I'm talking to you as a human, but an AI is listening to me and writing this post. At the same time, an AI is driving the car I'm in right now.

It's going to get messy.

But I'm in the "augmented human" camp: make humans better with AI. Why does it have to be humans versus AI?
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer advocates for 'augmented human' approach - using AI to enhance humans rather than framing it as humans vs AI. Notes he's dictating via AI while being driven by AI.
items.reasoning: Articulates a philosophical position on human-AI collaboration and gives a practical example of current AI integration in daily life.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer advocates for 'augmented human' approach - using AI to enhance humans rather than framing it as humans vs AI. Notes he's dictating via AI while being driven by AI.
items.content_html:
@kristofcreative What about the humans who use AI to do war?

For instance, I'm talking to you as a human, but an AI is listening to me and writing this post. At the same time, an AI is driving the car I'm in right now.

It's going to get messy.

But I'm in the "augmented human" camp: make humans better with AI. Why does it have to be humans versus AI?

items.title: @aem76us It is interesting that everyone is missing when you actually want to do that. 99% of the ti...
items.content:
@aem76us It is interesting that everyone is missing when you actually want to do that. 99% of the time it's when you are at a conference or an event when everyone is wearing a name badge. 

No need to go completely freaky, just do a name lookup of someone's badge. 

But, yeah, Zuckerberg has visions of everyone playing games with people they meet. 

There's a way to do this without freaking everyone out, is my point. We'll see who gets there.

Best way to learn:

Look at how other people solve their problems.

• The constraints they faced
• The tradeoffs they made
• The things they did to turn a demo into a system

Theory is nice and all, but case studies are 100x better.
Books are nice and all, but experience is 100x better.

items.title:
The most dangerous assumption in logistics?

That scaling a warehouse means hiring more people.

The...
items.content:
The most dangerous assumption in logistics?

That scaling a warehouse means hiring more people.

The smartest operators I’m seeing are doing the opposite, and Servo7 is a perfect example.

They’re building AI workforces that:

→ Pick
→ Pack
→ Process returns
→ Improve every single day

No breaks. No rebuilds. No downtime.

Here’s what’s really happening inside the next generation of warehouses.

items.title: Second, packaging and returns, historically the biggest friction points, are becoming autonomous dec...
items.content:
Second, packaging and returns, historically the biggest friction points, are becoming autonomous decision loops.

For packaging:

→ Measure
→ Size
→ Seal
→ Label
→ Handle any SKU or shape

For returns:

→ Open the box
→ Extract product
→ Inspect condition
→ Decide next action instantly

All integrated into existing warehouse systems.

No rebuild. No operational pause.

That integration piece is what most leaders underestimate.

items.title:
First, order picking is no longer manual labor. It’s computational strategy.

Servo7’s dual-arm robo...
items.content:
First, order picking is no longer manual labor. It’s computational strategy.

Servo7’s dual-arm robots:

→ See the item
→ Scan it
→ Grip it
→ Place it using a Tetris-style optimization algorithm

Every box is packed to maximize space and reduce shipping cost.

This is not just automation.

It’s algorithmic margin expansion.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon describes Servo7's dual-arm robots using Tetris-style optimization for order picking and packing.
items.reasoning: Interesting technical detail about algorithmic packing optimization, promotional but informative.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon describes Servo7's dual-arm robots using Tetris-style optimization for order picking and packing.
items.content_html:
First, order picking is no longer manual labor. It’s computational strategy.

Servo7’s dual-arm robots:

→ See the item

→ Scan it

→ Grip it

→ Place it using a Tetris-style optimization algorithm

Every box is packed to maximize space and reduce shipping cost.

This is not just automation.

It’s algorithmic margin expansion.

items.title: @huang_song_ @typelessdotcom I'm in my robot, which is driving me to San Francisco. It's a glorious ...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer gives an extensive review of Typeless voice-to-text app while being driven by Tesla FSD, calls it magical, wants it on Apple Vision Pro.
items.reasoning: Detailed product review showing real-world AI voice-to-text usage in autonomous driving context. Intersection of multiple AI technologies.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer gives an extensive review of Typeless voice-to-text app while being driven by Tesla FSD, calls it magical, wants it on Apple Vision Pro.
items.content_html:
@huang_song_ @typelessdotcom I'm in my robot, which is driving me to San Francisco. It's a glorious day, by the way. This product has completely changed how I use my Tesla; now I can look around, talk to Typeless, and it writes exactly what I'm talking about.

This is such a magical product. Every single post is better than what I said, which is funny because sometimes it does change my wording, but I look at it and think, "That's better than what I said." It remains accurate to my meaning, so thank you for fixing my mistakes and making my words look great. I have no idea how you train this, but it's quite special.

It is one of the best new things I've gotten in my life in a long time. It's not the sexiest—there's sexier stuff on my Apple Vision Pro—but by the way, I want you to build an app for the Apple Vision Pro. I have the same problem there, and the native voice system isn't nearly as good as this. I'd like to be able to talk to X on that platform as well.

items.title:
I like this kind of app.

This one is from @typelessdotcom, which I just updated to 1.0 on my iPhone...
items.content:
I like this kind of app.

This one is from @typelessdotcom, which I just updated to 1.0 on my iPhone.

I talk into my phone and Typeless changes it into words on your screen. I love this functionality.

If you're not using Typeless or its competitor, what are you doing? You're wasting your time. You're missing some magic. Get it? And it's still private.

And by the way, Apple, this is what you should have done with your keyboard. Where are you?

Great privacy statement. It would be nice if every company followed these rules.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer reviews Typeless 1.0 for iPhone voice-to-text, praises privacy, criticizes Apple for not building this into keyboard.
items.reasoning: Product review of AI voice tool with privacy angle and Apple criticism. Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer reviews Typeless 1.0 for iPhone voice-to-text, praises privacy, criticizes Apple for not building this into keyboard.
items.content_html:
I like this kind of app.

This one is from @typelessdotcom, which I just updated to 1.0 on my iPhone.

I talk into my phone and Typeless changes it into words on your screen. I love this functionality.

If you're not using Typeless or its competitor, what are you doing? You're wasting your time. You're missing some magic. Get it? And it's still private.

And by the way, Apple, this is what you should have done with your keyboard. Where are you?

Great privacy statement. It would be nice if every company followed these rules.

items.title: @huang_song_ @typelessdotcom Let's put it this way: I'll probably have Meta glasses, Google glasses,...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer discusses future of AR glasses from Meta, Google, Apple, and the dream of look-speak-understand interaction.
items.reasoning: Forward-looking view on AR/AI convergence but mostly speculative.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer discusses future of AR glasses from Meta, Google, Apple, and the dream of look-speak-understand interaction.
items.content_html:
@huang_song_ @typelessdotcom Let's put it this way: I'll probably have Meta glasses, Google glasses, Apple glasses, and probably a few other pairs. I already have Even Realities and Viture.

In two years, when Apple ships, I'm just going to use the Apple ones. Why? Because they'll have one little line inside, and their OS is freaking solid.

So, I have one dream: be able to look at something, speak, and have that thing understand. If you can make that happen in the Vision Pro today, you'll be set up for when the glasses arrive in two years.

items.title:
A math professor noticed his kitchen sink at home was leaking.

He called a plumber.

The plumber ca...
items.content:
A math professor noticed his kitchen sink at home was leaking.

He called a plumber.

The plumber came the next day, tightened a couple of nuts, and the sink worked perfectly again. The professor was delighted. But when, a minute later, the plumber handed him the bill, he was shocked.

“This is a third of my monthly salary!”

“Yeah, I get it…” said the plumber. “Why don’t you come work for our company as a plumber? You’ll make three times more than you do as a professor. Just remember: when you apply, say you only finished seventh grade. They don’t like hiring educated people.”

So the professor got a job as a plumber, and his life really did improve. All he had to do was tighten a nut here and there every so often, and his salary was much higher.

One day, the management of the plumbing company decided that every plumber had to attend evening classes to finish eighth grade. So our professor had to go too.

By chance, the very first class was math.

The evening school teacher, wanting to check what the students knew, asked for the formula for the area of a circle.

They called the professor up to the board, and he suddenly realized he’d forgotten it. He started frantically reasoning it out, covering the board with integrals, differentials, and all sorts of fancy formulas to re-derive the result. In the end, he got:

S = –π r²

He didn’t like the minus sign, so he started again.
Again he got a minus. No matter what he did, it kept coming out negative.

He cast a panicked look at the class, and all the plumbers were whispering:

“Swap the limits of integration!”
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov shares a math/plumber joke about integration limits, which went mega-viral.
items.reasoning: Entertaining math joke, extremely viral (757K views, 6.6K likes) but not AI-related.
items.summary_html: Burkov shares a math/plumber joke about integration limits, which went mega-viral.

“This is a third of my monthly salary!”

“Yeah, I get it…” said the plumber. “Why don’t you come work for our company as a plumber? You’ll make three times more than you do as a professor. Just remember: when you apply, say you only finished seventh grade. They don’t like hiring educated people.”

So the professor got a job as a plumber, and his life really did improve. All he had to do was tighten a nut here and there every so often, and his salary was much higher.

One day, the management of the plumbing company decided that every plumber had to attend evening classes to finish eighth grade. So our professor had to go too.

By chance, the very first class was math.

The evening school teacher, wanting to check what the students knew, asked for the formula for the area of a circle.

They called the professor up to the board, and he suddenly realized he’d forgotten it. He started frantically reasoning it out, covering the board with integrals, differentials, and all sorts of fancy formulas to re-derive the result. In the end, he got:

He didn’t like the minus sign, so he started again.

Again he got a minus. No matter what he did, it kept coming out negative.

He cast a panicked look at the class, and all the plumbers were whispering:

items.title:
As always I'm pro-immigration

I've been an immigrant myself for the last 17 years and I'm proud to ...
items.content:
As always I'm pro-immigration

I've been an immigrant myself for the last 17 years and I'm proud to be one

I don't have a criminal record, I've paid millions in taxes, spent millions, tried to learn the language and culture (especially now in Portugal) and assimilate as much as possible

First in Korea, then the US, then all over Asia, then all over Europe and parts of Latin America and now in Portugal

But immigration should in most cases be a net positive to society, socially, economically and culturally

With a small pre-set quotum of Good Samaritan where you bring in refugees etc to help them out, but with a limit

There's no limits now and there's no assesment at all of potential contribution of immigrants to society, and it's deemed racist in Europe to even suggest that

But unlike immigration that's unlimited, government finances are in fact limited, and when the money runs out to pay for everyone you see what happens, higher taxes, the high skilled and wealthy leave first, and the country will increasingly get poorer

You see that happen now in Western and Southern Europe

There's no real positive end game I can imagine except social strife and maybe civil war

Very very risky
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio's long post on immigration policy views as an immigrant himself, discussing net positive immigration, limits, and concerns about Western Europe
items.reasoning: High engagement (481 likes) but completely off-topic for AI/ML analysis. Levelsio's views on immigration policy.
items.summary_html: Levelsio's long post on immigration policy views as an immigrant himself, discussing net positive immigration, limits, and concerns about Western Europe
items.content_html:
As always I'm pro-immigration

I've been an immigrant myself for the last 17 years and I'm proud to be one

I don't have a criminal record, I've paid millions in taxes, spent millions, tried to learn the language and culture (especially now in Portugal) and assimilate as much as possible

First in Korea, then the US, then all over Asia, then all over Europe and parts of Latin America and now in Portugal

But immigration should in most cases be a net positive to society, socially, economically and culturally

With a small pre-set quotum of Good Samaritan where you bring in refugees etc to help them out, but with a limit

There's no limits now and there's no assesment at all of potential contribution of immigrants to society, and it's deemed racist in Europe to even suggest that

But unlike immigration that's unlimited, government finances are in fact limited, and when the money runs out to pay for everyone you see what happens, higher taxes, the high skilled and wealthy leave first, and the country will increasingly get poorer

You see that happen now in Western and Southern Europe

There's no real positive end game I can imagine except social strife and maybe civil war

Very very risky

items.title: @AndreaDiPrata "The overwhelming majority of the population of European countries is made up of nati...
items.content:
@AndreaDiPrata "The overwhelming majority of the population of European countries is made up of native people."

This is pretty much false

Germany's population with a migrant background is now around 30%

For Sweden it's 35% non-Swedish
Netherlands: 22%
France: 17%

In London it's 63%!

There's nothing "overwhelming majority" about those, in fact they're pretty close to 50% of the population

Once the share gets I'd say over 10%, you quickly lose your own culture

As an immigrant I know this all too well
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio long thread on European immigration demographics and cultural assimilation
items.reasoning: Off-topic immigration discussion, no AI/ML relevance despite being from a tech figure.
